message.content: Adversaries may exploit XDG autostart entries to execute programs or commands during system boot on Linux desktop environments that are XDG compliant. These entries, stored in /etc/xdg/autostart or ~/.config/autostart with a .desktop file extension, allow applications to start automatically after user logon. By modifying these entries, adversaries can maintain persistence by executing malicious commands or payloads, such as remote access tools, during desktop environment startup. The entries specify the type of application, an arbitrary name, and the command to execute. Common exploitation methods include using Masquerading to make malicious entries appear legitimate. Notable malware like Fysbis and NETWIRE have used XDG autostart entries for persistence. Mitigation strategies include monitoring and validating the integrity of autostart directories and files, employing application whitelisting, and using endpoint detection and response (EDR) tools to detect and block suspicious activities. Regular audits and user education on recognizing unauthorized changes can also help in early detection and prevention.
